Keywords: clear, defined, data-available, bounded, quantitative, optimization, calculable
Characteristics: Clear problem definition; Known variables; Quantifiable outcomes; Established methods exist; Data is available; Solution can be verified
Recommended Approach: Direct analytical approach; Standard issue tree decomposition; Quantitative analysis focus; Benchmark against known solutions
Decomposition Style: Issue tree with quantitative branches; Factor decomposition; Mathematical modeling
Analysis Methods: Regression analysis; Benchmarking; Sensitivity analysis; Optimization modeling; Statistical testing
Common Mistakes: Over-complicating the analysis; Ignoring simple heuristics; Not validating assumptions; Spending too long on framing
Example Domains: Cost optimization; Process improvement; Pricing strategy; Resource allocation; Inventory management

Keywords: ambiguous, complex, multi-stakeholder, qualitative, judgment, uncertain, emerging
Characteristics: Problem definition unclear; Multiple valid framings; Mix of quantitative and qualitative; Stakeholder disagreement; Partial data available; Multiple possible solutions
Recommended Approach: Hypothesis-driven approach; Multiple decomposition attempts; Iterative framing; Stakeholder interviews; Scenario analysis
Decomposition Style: Hypothesis tree with early pruning; Multiple cleaving frames tested; Combine quantitative and qualitative branches
Analysis Methods: Hypothesis testing; Expert interviews; Analogical reasoning; Scenario planning; Decision trees; Monte Carlo simulation
Common Mistakes: Anchoring on first framing; Not exploring alternative decompositions; Ignoring qualitative evidence; Premature convergence on a solution
Example Domains: Market entry strategy; Organizational redesign; New product development; M&A evaluation; Partnership strategy

Keywords: systemic, interconnected, no-right-answer, evolving, political, adaptive, emergent
Characteristics: No definitive formulation; No stopping rule; Solutions are not true/false but better/worse; Every attempt changes the problem; No ultimate test; Stakeholders have conflicting values; Problem is unique
Recommended Approach: Iterative and adaptive approach; Design thinking integration; Stakeholder co-creation; Prototype and test; Embrace uncertainty; Use multiple frameworks simultaneously
Decomposition Style: Multiple parallel decompositions; Systems mapping; Causal loop diagrams; Stakeholder value mapping; Intervention points identification
Analysis Methods: Systems dynamics; Design thinking; Rapid prototyping; Stakeholder workshops; Agent-based modeling; Real options analysis
Common Mistakes: Treating it like a well-structured problem; Seeking a 'perfect' solution; Ignoring political dynamics; Top-down solutions without buy-in; Linear planning for non-linear problems
Example Domains: Climate change strategy; Healthcare system reform; Urban planning; Education reform; Poverty reduction; Digital transformation

Keywords: root cause, failure, investigation, forensic, troubleshooting, defect, performance-gap
Characteristics: Something went wrong or performance is declining; Need to find root cause; Historical data available; Multiple potential causes; Time pressure to resolve
Recommended Approach: Root cause analysis; Fishbone (Ishikawa) decomposition; 5 Whys; Fault tree analysis; Process of elimination
Decomposition Style: Fishbone diagram; Fault tree (top-down); 5 Whys chain; Process flow breakdown; Timeline decomposition
Analysis Methods: Pareto analysis; Correlation analysis; A/B testing; Process mapping; Statistical process control; Failure mode analysis
Common Mistakes: Jumping to conclusions; Fixing symptoms not causes; Confirmation bias in investigation; Not considering multiple root causes; Stopping at first plausible explanation
Example Domains: Quality defects; Customer churn; System failures; Performance degradation; Safety incidents; Revenue decline

Keywords: growth, innovation, new-market, expansion, investment, value-creation, strategic
Characteristics: No crisis but seeking growth; Multiple options available; Resource allocation decision; Risk-reward tradeoffs; Future-oriented; Competitive landscape matters
Recommended Approach: Portfolio approach; Option comparison framework; Risk-reward analysis; Scenario planning; Stage-gate evaluation
Decomposition Style: Decision tree (options as branches); Criteria-based evaluation; Portfolio matrix; Risk-return decomposition
Analysis Methods: NPV/ROI analysis; Competitive analysis; Market sizing; Customer research; Real options valuation; SWOT analysis
Common Mistakes: Anchoring on one option too early; Ignoring opportunity cost; Underestimating risks; Not testing assumptions; Sunk cost fallacy
Example Domains: New market entry; Product portfolio; Capital allocation; Innovation pipeline; Partnership evaluation; Geographic expansion

Keywords: create, build, new, novel, invention, user-centered, solution-seeking, prototype
Characteristics: Need to create something new; User needs must be understood; Multiple solution paths; Iteration required; Technical feasibility unknowns; Aesthetic and functional requirements
Recommended Approach: Design thinking process; User research first; Rapid prototyping; Iterative testing; Cross-functional collaboration
Decomposition Style: User journey mapping; Functional decomposition; Constraint mapping; Solution space exploration; Prototype-test-iterate cycle
Analysis Methods: User research; Prototyping; Usability testing; Technical feasibility assessment; Competitive teardown; Concept testing
Common Mistakes: Designing without user research; Over-engineering v1; Not testing early enough; Ignoring constraints; Feature creep
Example Domains: Product design; Service design; Experience design; System architecture; Process design; Business model design

Keywords: forecast, estimate, projection, future, trend, probability, scenario, modeling
Characteristics: Need to estimate future outcomes; Historical data may or may not be relevant; Uncertainty is inherent; Multiple variables interact; Accuracy expectations vary
Recommended Approach: Triangulation of methods; Base rates plus adjustment; Scenario building; Sensitivity analysis; Bayesian updating
Decomposition Style: Driver decomposition; Scenario tree; Component estimation; Top-down vs bottom-up decomposition
Analysis Methods: Time series analysis; Regression modeling; Expert elicitation; Monte Carlo simulation; Bayesian estimation; Analogy-based estimation
Common Mistakes: Over-precision; Ignoring base rates; Single-point estimates without ranges; Anchoring on recent data; Not updating estimates with new information
Example Domains: Market sizing; Financial forecasting; Demand planning; Risk assessment; Technology adoption; Election prediction

Keywords: conflict, stakeholder, alignment, compromise, win-win, mediation, agreement
Characteristics: Multiple parties with different interests; Resource or value to be divided; Relationships matter; Information asymmetry; Zone of possible agreement (ZOPA) exists
Recommended Approach: Interest-based analysis; BATNA assessment; Zone of possible agreement mapping; Value creation before claiming; Principled negotiation framework
Decomposition Style: Stakeholder interest decomposition; Issue linkage mapping; Value creation tree; BATNA tree; Package option generation
Analysis Methods: Stakeholder analysis; Game theory; Interest mapping; Package evaluation; Fairness analysis; Precedent research
Common Mistakes: Positional bargaining; Win-lose framing; Not preparing BATNA; Ignoring relationship dynamics; Anchoring too aggressively
Example Domains: Business partnerships; Labor relations; International diplomacy; Contract negotiation; Cross-team resource allocation; Vendor management
